maintainer_org: agno-agi (formerly Phidata Inc.; the framework was rebranded from `phidata` to `agno` in 2024 as the project pivoted from a data-tooling-adjacent agent library to a general-purpose agent framework)
license: Apache-2.0 (Apache License 2.0; SDK is OSS while AgentOS control-plane appears partly commercial)
execution_kernel_pattern: Single-agent control-loop kernel: an `Agent(model=..., tools=..., instructions=..., memory=..., knowledge=..., storage=...)` object exposes `agent.run()` / `agent.print_response()` / async + streaming variants; docs describe an agent as 'a stateful control loop around a stateless model — the model reasons and calls tools in a loop, guided by instructions.' Above the single-agent kernel, Agno layers two orthogonal composites: `Team` (coordinator/coordinate/route/collaborate modes over member agents) and `Workflow` (deterministic step-based orchestration). In v2 the runtime also exposes the same agents as a service via AgentOS, but the in-process kernel remains the canonical Agent loop.

multi_agent_support.style: Two-tier: (a) Team — a parent agent that dispatches to member agents in coordinate / route / collaborate / hand-off modes (LLM-driven dispatch); (b) Workflow — step-based deterministic orchestration with conditional branches, loops, and parallel steps. Dynamic team/agent factories allow per-request instantiation.
multi_agent_support.notes: Agno explicitly positions teams as 'level 4' agentic systems and workflows as 'level 5' in its earlier framing. Coordination is mostly LLM-mediated (handoff via tool calls or coordinator agent) — closer to OpenAI Swarm / CrewAI than to AutoGen's GroupChat conversation loop.

tool_protocol: Function-calling via Python callables decorated/wrapped as tools; first-class MCP client (`MCPTools`) for connecting to external MCP servers; AgentOS can itself be exposed AS an MCP server. Ships ~100+ pre-built toolkits (Slack, Drive, web search, SQL, code execution, etc.). Supports tool call compression, filtering, human-in-the-loop confirmations, and external execution.
memory_model: Three-layer memory model — explicitly emphasized as a differentiator: (1) session storage (chat history persisted per session, multiple DB backends — Postgres, SQLite, Mongo, etc.); (2) user memory via a `MemoryManager` that extracts and stores facts about the user across sessions (long-term memory); (3) agentic session state — dictionaries the agent can read/write during a run. Knowledge (RAG over vector stores) is a separate primitive. The 'agents remember users across sessions' story is one of Agno's loudest differentiators vs LangChain/LlamaIndex defaults.
supported_model_providers: OpenAI, Anthropic (Claude), Google (Gemini), Meta (Llama), Mistral, Cohere, Groq, Together, Fireworks, DeepSeek, xAI, Azure OpenAI, AWS Bedrock, Ollama, vLLM, HuggingFace, plus litellm bridge — ~25+ providers as of v2. Provider abstraction is unified under an `agno.models.*` namespace.
observability_story: OpenTelemetry tracing out of the box; AgentOS exposes run history, audit logs, token counts, context-compression metrics, RBAC, and a tracing UI. Evaluation primitives are first-class: Accuracy Evals (LLM-as-judge vs gold), Agent-as-Judge Evals, Performance Evals (latency + memory footprint), Reliability Evals (tool-call correctness). This eval-as-first-class posture is unusual among peers.
typical_use_case: Production assistant agents (in-product copilots, Slack/Telegram/Discord bots), data labeling and synthetic data generation, RAG agents with persistent user memory, multi-agent research teams, scheduled/cron'd background agents — all targeted at teams that want to self-host the full stack (sessions + memory + knowledge + traces in their own DB).
maturity_signal: Strong and rapidly growing — ~40k stars (passed CrewAI in 2025), v2 launched late 2025 with AgentOS, active weekly releases (currently v2.6.x), ~99% Python, Apache-2.0. Adoption signal: marketed primarily to product/ML/AI teams at companies rather than to research hobbyists. The framework benchmarked itself aggressively in 2024–2025 with widely-cited claims of ~3μs agent instantiation and ~6.5 KiB memory footprint per agent (vs reportedly orders of magnitude more for LangGraph) — methodology is open-sourced under `cookbook/agent_concepts/other/performance.py` but the comparison was contested as cold-instantiation-only (not full run-time). Current v2 docs have de-emphasized the headline benchmark; perf is now framed via the Performance Evals primitive instead.
one_paragraph_differentiator: Agno (formerly Phidata, rebranded 2024) is a general-purpose Python agent framework whose kernel is a single-Agent stateful control loop — superficially convergent with LangChain's AgentExecutor or LlamaIndex's AgentRunner — but distinguished by four deliberate bets: (1) long-term user memory as a first-class primitive (MemoryManager extracting facts across sessions, persisted in user's own DB) rather than a bolt-on; (2) eval-as-first-class with four orthogonal eval modes (accuracy / agent-as-judge / performance / reliability) baked into the SDK; (3) an explicit 5-levels-of-agentic-systems taxonomy (1-tools, 2-knowledge, 3-memory, 4-teams, 5-workflows) used as a pedagogical and API-shape ladder — closer to a curriculum than to a research taxonomy; (4) a full self-hostable runtime (AgentOS) sitting above the SDK with REST/SSE/WebSocket APIs, RBAC, cron, and OpenTelemetry, positioning Agno as the 'own-your-stack' alternative to managed services like OpenAI Assistants. Vs the peer set: vs LangChain — more opinionated, smaller surface area, memory/eval/runtime are integrated rather than ecosystem-of-packages; vs LlamaIndex — Agno is agent-first not retrieval-first (knowledge is a primitive but not the center of gravity); vs openai-agents-sdk — Agno is provider-pluralist and ships a memory/runtime layer; vs pydantic-ai — Agno is heavier and platform-shaped where pydantic-ai is type-safety-shaped. Kernel-position verdict: convergent kernel (stateful loop) but distinct platform envelope — the differentiation is at the layer above the kernel, not at the kernel itself.
known_anti_patterns: (1) Benchmark methodology in 2024–2025 was instantiation-time-only, which critics noted is not the dominant cost in a real agent run dominated by LLM latency — credible but easy to over-read; (2) v2 README has shifted heavily toward enterprise/platform marketing language ('own your stack', 'RBAC', 'audit trails') and away from concrete code-shape, making the kernel harder to evaluate from the landing page alone; (3) the 5-levels taxonomy is a marketing/pedagogical device, not a research-grounded classification — useful as an API ladder, but should not be cited as a peer-reviewed framework; (4) AgentOS introduces a partial open-core split that is not yet fully transparent — verify license boundary before adopting for production.
evidence_sources: https://github.com/agno-agi/agno — repo metadata, stars, license, language, last release
https://docs.agno.com/llms.txt — documentation index (yielded the eval categories, MCP support, memory architecture)
https://docs.agno.com/agents/introduction — kernel framing: 'stateful control loop around a stateless model'

Prior-knowledge: Agno's 2024–2025 benchmark posts and the Phidata→Agno rebrand are well-documented in community threads (HN, Reddit r/LocalLLaMA) and were referenced repeatedly in the v1 README — current v2 README has been rewritten so those claims are no longer surfaced verbatim.
